diagrams.description: This timeline traces the development of Cultural Studies from its pre‑history to the present. Key milestones include: the early influences of Raymond Williams’s ‘Culture and Society’ (1958) and Richard Hoggart’s ‘The Uses of Literacy’ (1957); the founding of the Birmingham Centre for Contemporary Cultural Studies (CCCS) in 1964 under Hoggart and later Stuart Hall; the development of key theoretical concepts such as encoding/decoding (Hall, 1973) and the analysis of subcultures (Resistance through Rituals, 1976); the adoption of Gramsci’s hegemony and Althusser’s ideology in the 1970s; the feminist and postcolonial turns in the 1980s with works by Angela McRobbie and Paul Gilroy’s ‘There Ain’t No Black in the Union Jack’ (1987); the rise of cultural policy studies and the critique of postmodernism in the 1990s; and the expansion into digital culture, globalisation, and affect theory in the 2000s‑2020s.
